metadata.title: Impossibility Theorems: The Skeptic's Most Useful Result
metadata.note: CLI video: B00 intro · B01 PROBLEM · B02 ASK · B03 CODE · B04 OUTPUT(manim) · B05 CHANGE · B06–B08 slates · B09 outro

beats.narration_text: Nik Bear Brown. Build it with a CLI, then take it apart.

Arrow proved no fair voting rule exists. Balinski and Young proved no fair apportionment avoids all paradoxes. These theorems are the skeptic's most useful result: they tell you exactly which trade-off you cannot escape.
beats.visual_intent: title card: 'Impossibility theorems: they tell you which trade-off you cannot escape'

beats.narration_text: Can Claude explain what Arrow's theorem and Balinski-Young's theorem say, and show how the same impossibility structure applies to ML fairness?

props.command: claude "Explain Arrow's impossibility theorem (1951) and Balinski-Young (1982). List the three criteria each proves cannot be simultaneously satisfied. Give one real documented case for each. Then show the analogous three-way tension in ML fairness metrics."

beats.narration_text: Arrow 1951: Non-Dictator, Pareto, and Independence of Irrelevant Alternatives cannot all hold. Real case: the spoiler effect in the 2000 US election. Balinski-Young 1982: Quota Rule, Monotonicity, and No Paradox cannot all hold. Real case: the Alabama paradox in the 1880 Census.

Two triangles, two theorems, one structure. Arrow on the left: three fairness criteria at the vertices, a crimson X at the center — you cannot have all three. Balinski-Young on the right: same structure, different domain.
beats.visual_intent: Manim two equilateral triangles side by side: Arrow's theorem left, Balinski-Young right, each with 3 criteria at vertices and CRIMSON X at center

beats.narration_text: The extension to ML: accuracy, equalized odds, and calibration form the same triangular trade-off. COMPAS chose calibration — and the accuracy and equalized-odds failures followed by theorem.

props.command: claude "For COMPAS recidivism: show which of the three ML fairness criteria it optimized, which two it violated, and whether the Chouldechova 2017 proof applies."

beats.narration_text: Chouldechova 2017 proved it formally: when base rates differ across groups, you cannot simultaneously have equal false-positive rates and equal false-negative rates and calibration. COMPAS violated two by satisfying the third — not by accident, by theorem.
beats.visual_intent: slate: Chouldechova 2017 — the three-way COMPAS impossibility proof

beats.narration_text: Impossibility theorems are the skeptic's most useful tool: they tell you which failure is structurally guaranteed before you deploy. The question is not whether to accept a trade-off — it is which one.
beats.visual_intent: recap card: 'Impossibility theorem -> which trade-off you cannot escape, before you deploy'

beats.narration_text: Your move: for any AI system optimizing multiple fairness criteria, identify the impossibility theorem that applies and state explicitly which criterion you are sacrificing and why.
beats.visual_intent: next-steps card: 1) identify the applicable impossibility theorem, 2) state which criterion is sacrificed, 3) document the choice

beats.narration_text: Nik Bear Brown. Build it with a CLI. Then take it apart. At Nik Bear Brown, nikbearbrown dot com.
